utterances: They have a couple more days here possibly; today's the formal end, but they might go on into Saturday.
And they're going to try to at least agree to something, even informal, that they'll continue talks about a world after 2012.

What's most important is trying to get the big countries that are not members of the Kyoto protocol now--the United States, but also China and India, which will be large emitters of greenhouse gases--somehow, some way to get them involved.
NPR's Chris Joyce in Montreal at the climate conference.
Chris, thank you.
You're welcome.
Let me just note there's a new feature at our Web site, npr.org.
It's called World Opinion.
If you go there and click on, you can see what--there is other comment from around the world about the Kyoto protocols on greenhouse gas emissions.
The shouting matches this week on cable TV had a military air.
(Soundbite of argument on TV show ""Hardball with Chris Matthews"")
Pete Hegseth and Jon Soltz, here on MSNBC's ""Hardball with Chris Matthews,"" arguing about whether former General Wesley Clark went too far in attacking John McCain's military record.
Hegseth and Soltz lead opposing veteran's organizations - one conservative, one liberal - in a year when veterans may have an unusually strong political voice.
NPR's Peter Overby reports.
VoteVets.org, that's Jon Soltz's group, made its reputation two years ago.
It endorsed several veterans of Iraq and Afghanistan who ran for Congress.
And it aired this much-discussed ad against several Republican senators.
A veteran fires an AK-47 at a modern body-armor vest and a military-issue vest.
(Soundbite of commercial)
(Soundbite of gunshots)
Unidentified Actor #1: The difference is life.
(Soundbite of velcro unfastening)
Unidentified Actor #1: Or death.
Unidentified Actor #2: Senator Conrad Burns voted against giving our troops this.
Unidentified Actor #3: Now it's time for us to vote against him.
Now, VoteVets is back on TV, this time criticizing John McCain for opposing a new GI bill.
(Soundbite of commercial)
Unidentified Actor #4: McCain thinks covering a fraction of our education is enough.
Unidentified Actor #5: We didn't give a fraction in Iraq.
We gave 100 percent.
Unidentified Actor #4: It's time for some straight talk.
VoteVets has always had a liberal tilt.
It's against the surge, against the Iraq war, but not against all wars.
VoteVets chairman, Jon Soltz.
I would call it pro-defeating al-Qaeda.
In the past month, we've had more casualties and deaths in Afghanistan than we had Iraq.
We have a situation where Osama bin Laden is still on the loose.
But VoteVets isn't the only voice for America's newest veterans.
Another group, Vets for Freedom, say they want to defeat al-Qaeda in Iraq.
Executive Director Joel Arends says this group has just one litmus test for supporting candidates.
And that litmus test is, do you support the surge in Iraq?
Do you understand how that relates to national security in this country?
So Vets for Freedom has its own ads online.
Here a wounded veteran of the Illinois National Guard says he and some other veterans tried to meet with their home state senator, Barack Obama.
But he wasn't available.
(Soundbite of commercial)
Unidentified Actor #6: Yeah, he's willing to travel to Iran to meet with their leader, or anyone else who hates our country.
The question for America is if Barack Obama won't listen to us, who will he listen to?
Information is sketchy about financing for both groups here.
That's because they both have political action committees which disclose their donors and 501(c)(4) advocacy arms which don't.
Quin Monson researches political money at Brigham Young University.
The fact that they've chosen to act as (c)(4)s indicates that they don't want anyone to know what they're doing, and that the donors probably don't want people to know either.

What we do know is that the VoteVets donor list includes some familiar liberal names: powerful unions and Democratic politicians, plus the big donors group Democracy Alliance, and another liberal financing group, Fund for America, and MoveOn.org.
There is less information on Vets for Freedom.
It did get 623,000 dollars last year from Freedom's Watch.
That's a conservative organization, largely underwritten by casino magnate Sheldon Adelson.
Four years ago, one veterans group had a huge impact, Swift Boat Veterans for Truth, which attacked democrat John Kerry.
It's unclear if a vets' group could have that kind of effect this year.
But Vanderbilt University Professor John Geer who studies negative advertising says a combat veteran who is anti-Iraq, a VoteVets kind of veteran, could be seen as the most plausible voice.
First time I saw McCune was in what's called a nut row.
He was inside his cell.
His fingers were completely stained with tobacco.
However, good days have been pretty rare for Gibbons over the last few weeks.
First there were the allegations that he grabbed a woman in a parking garage after they'd been drinking together.
Then, charges he hired an illegal immigrant as a nanny.
And most recently, a Wall Street Journal story alleging he used his influence in Congress to steer millions of dollars in military contracts towards a friend.
Gibbons has denied all the allegations, which he calls dirty tricks.
All of the things within the last couple of weeks, it all seems to be directed toward character assassination right before the election.
And certainly it was an effort to accomplish a change in the political outcome.
Whether or not that's true, one thing does seem to be clear: Jim Gibbons' PR nightmare got its start, appropriately enough, on Friday, October 13th.
It was about 10:00 p.m. in a parking lot outside a McCormick and Schmick's restaurant near the Vegas strip.
(Soundbite of 911 Emergency Call)
Unidentified Woman #1 (911 Operator): 911 Emergency (unintelligible).
Hi.
My name is Christy Mazzeo.
And...
Christy Mazzeo, a 32-year-old single mother and cocktail waitress, made a series of 911 calls the night she claims Gibbons came on to her in the parking garage.
He's denied he did anything wrong, but the story has been a local obsession around Nevada.
And it's safe to say the beneficiary of all the drama has been Gibbons' opponent, State Senator Dina Titus.
Oh, my goodness.
I never thought I would be in the Coliseum of Caesar's Palace with Dina Titus signs and people here.
This is fabulous.
This is Las Vegas, isn't it?
(Soundbite of cheering)
Yesterday, as Mr. Bush was stumping for Republicans up in Elko, Titus was in Las Vegas with a president of her own - Bill Clinton.
I was accused of coming here for Dina Titus because, to my ears, she's one of the few politicians out here without an accent.
(Soundbite of laughter)
Clinton's appearance was just another sign of how winnable Democrats think this race is.
A month ago, Gibbons seemed unbeatable.
Now, it's a toss-up.
Titus says she plans to press as much flesh as possible in the waning days of the campaign.
I'm going to job sites, I'm going to shift changes, I'm going to churches, shopping centers.
I don't want to be sitting at home.
I want to be out there looking people in the eye and asking them for their vote.
Jan Meyer(ph) had just cast her vote yesterday at an early polling station off of Sahara Boulevard when I caught up with her.
She said she'd lost respect for Gibbons over the allegations, but had still voted for him.
Because I still think, as much as I don't like him, I think he's still going to be better than she will be.
